**The Lighthouse, Dunmore Head**  
**12th of April, 1879**  

*To My Dearest Eliza,*  

I pen these words in the dim light of the lantern room, the sea howling like a wounded beast outside. The storm is upon us, and the wind beats against the glass like a thousand fists. I pray the heavens may spare us, for I fear my strength is waning with each passing hour.  

Five years have passed since you left this house, and in all that time, I have heard nary a word from you. I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink out and return, and yet still, your name has not crossed my lips. I have been alone in this lighthouse, save for the sea, the wind, and the occasional seabird that dares to perch upon the rocks.  

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. I have not written to you. I have not even thought of you. But now, as the storm gathers strength and the lantern flickers with each gust, I feel the weight of years pressing upon my soul. I have been a fool, and I have been a coward.  

I do not know if I will survive this night, but if I do, I will not be the same man. I will not be the one who turned his back on the one thing that ever mattered to me.  

You were

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old and unrelenting companion, roars with a fury I have not heard in many a year. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I know it may not be enough to guide any ship through the night. But I do it anyway, as I have done for thirty years now, and I suppose it is my way of keeping the world from forgetting me.

I have not written to you in five years, and I do not know if you will ever read this. But I must try. You were once the light of my life, and though time has dimmed the glow, I still feel the ache of your absence. I have seen your name in the ledger of the coastguard, and I have heard the stories of your life in the city, of your work, your friends, your choices. I do not judge you, Eleanor. I only wish you had come to see me once more.

Tonight, the storm is upon us. I have no doubt it will be the last. I have been a lighthouse keeper for longer than I have lived in this house, and I have seen many storms. But this one feels different. The waves are higher, the wind is colder, and I fear the light may not hold. If I do not survive

Ah, my dear child, if you're reading these words, then perhaps the storm has claimed me, or perhaps you've come to find me in the lighthouse, as I have always been. Either way, I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and now, with the wind howling like a vengeful spirit and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the world, I feel the weight of silence pressing upon me like the very stones of this tower.

You were never meant to be the daughter of a lighthouse keeper, my dear. You were meant for the world beyond this rock, for the grand cities, the bustling streets, the life of a woman with a purpose, a place, a future. And I, in my stubbornness, held you back. I clung to the light, to the solitude, to the quiet certainty of the sea, and I forgot that you were not the sea. You were not meant to be bound to these walls, these endless nights, this eternal vigil.

But now, as the storm gathers, I see the truth. I see that I have been wrong. That I have been a fool. You were never meant to be here, and I was never meant to be the one who kept you from the world. I was the one who kept myself from it, and in doing so, I kept you from the life you were meant to have.

I have spent five years in regret, in

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may come too late. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have found myself adrift in a sea of regret and sorrow. Tonight, as I write these words, a storm is brewing on the horizon — a storm that may claim my life, and I want you to know that I have never stopped thinking of you.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even in the darkest hours of my life, your presence was a beacon. I can still remember the day you left — the way your eyes filled with tears, the way your voice trembled as you said you had to go. I tried to reach you, but the distance between us was too great, and the silence that followed was heavier than the silence of the sea.

I have not spoken to you in all this time, and I know I have done wrong. I never meant to hurt you, and I have not stopped praying for the day when we can be reconciled. I have been a failure as a father, and I carry that weight like a stone on my chest. But I want you to know that I have never stopped loving you, and I would give anything to have you back in my life.

Tonight, as the storm approaches, I find myself wondering if this will be the end. I have always believed that the light of the lighthouse is a promise — a promise that

**To My Dearest and Most Beloved Eliza,**

**Oft-times I have penned this letter, and oft-times I have laid down my quill in sorrow, for the ink seems but a pale shadow of the love that dwells within my heart. Yet now, as the winds howl like the very devils of the sea and the sky is black as a beggar's cloak, I must write, for I fear that I may not live to see another dawn.**

**The storm that now gathers upon the horizon is of a most terrible kind, as though the heavens themselves are in wrath. The waves, they rise like mountains, and the wind doth howl with a voice that shakes the very bones of a man. I have stood upon the cliff, my lamp alight, and have seen the sea in a fury that would daunt even the bravest of sailors. I fear that the lighthouse may not stand, and with it, my life. Yet I have seen the light, and I have seen the stars, and I have faith that the Lord shall guide me through this trial.**

**But I write not of the storm, though it is a matter of great import. I write of you, my dear Eliza, and of the sorrow that has driven us apart. I have not seen you for five long years, and I have heard naught from thy lips since the day thou didst leave our home in London. I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the distance between us is measured in miles, it feels as though it stretches across an ocean of unspoken words and unmet expectations.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and of quiet resolve. You know this. You were raised to understand the weight of responsibility, the solemnity of purpose. And yet, I fear that in my steadfastness, I have failed you. I have failed to be the father you deserved, and I have failed to be the man you once believed in.

Tonight, the storm is coming. The skies have darkened with a fury that seems almost divine, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with a voice that shakes the very foundations of this lighthouse. I have spent my life guarding this beacon, ensuring that no soul is lost to the dark. But tonight, I fear that even the light may not be enough.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The winds howl like wolves, and the waves crash with a vengeance that has not been seen in decades. I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and I have never known such a storm. But I have known loneliness, and I have known regret. And I have known the ache of not being there when you needed me

The wind howls like a banshee in the night, and the sea roars with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I’ve spent more than thirty years on this rock, and I’ve seen storms of every kind—sudden, violent, and cruel—but never have I felt so alone as I do now, with the sky black as a thief’s hand and the lighthouse light flickering like a dying soul.

I’ve been up here for hours, watching the waves crash against the cliffs, and I’ve thought of you more than I have in five years. I’ve said nothing, not because I don’t care, but because I’ve never known what to say. You left me with no explanation, no warning, and no time to say goodbye. I’ve tried to forget you, but the wind carries your name across the sea, and the tide brings it back to me again and again.

I’ve always been a man of silence, of duty, of the sea. But tonight, I’ve no more strength to keep my mouth shut. You were my only child, and I’ve spent more than half my life watching over you, even from a distance. I’ve watched you grow, and I’ve watched you turn your back on me. I don’t know if you still remember me, or if you’ve even thought of me at all. But I’ve not forgotten you, and I never will.

The storm is coming, and I’m not sure I

*April 15th, 1897*

My dear Eliza,

I've held this pen in my hand for hours, trying to find the right words. But the wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as if it's trying to drown me. I can't write the way I want to. Not yet.

Five years. That's how long it's been since you left. Five years since I watched you walk out of this house, your back turned, your eyes full of fire and fury. I've tried to forget, but I can't. Not when I hear your name whispered in the wind, or when the light flickers and I think I hear your voice in the silence.

I know you think I'm a monster. That I abandoned you. But you don't understand. I had to. I had to keep the light burning. You don't know what it's like to be alone on this rock, with nothing but the sea and the sky. You don't know what it's like to watch the world go dark and know that if the light goes out, people die. I had to choose between you and the people who depend on me. I chose the light.

But tonight... tonight is different. The storm is coming. I've seen the sky. It's the kind of storm that doesn't let up. The kind that takes everything. I don't know if I'll make it through. And if

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit at my window, and the sea roaring as though it too mourns the silence between us. This night is not like the others. The stars are obscured, the waves grow fiercer, and I fear that this may be the last night I shall see the light of day. But I must write, for you are my only hope of mending what time and distance have torn asunder.

Five long years have passed since your last letter, and I have waited, day after day, for the ink of your words to return to me. Yet you have not written, nor have I heard a word from you since the day you left. I have tried to forgive, to forget, to believe that you were simply lost, as all children are in the great, unknowable world. But the silence has grown heavier, and I fear that I have not only lost you, but my own soul in the process.

I know you think me a cruel man, perhaps even a monster, for I have not sought you out, nor have I begged for your return. But I have not been cruel, Eliza. I have been afraid. Afraid that if I spoke, if I reached for you, you would vanish again, like the mist that curls at the edge of the sea. I have held the lantern in my hands, day and night, not just to

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**From the Lighthouse at Storm’s End**

**Upon the cliffs of the Western Reach**

**I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of silence.**

Five years have passed since you left, and though the sea has not forgotten me, I have forgotten how to speak your name without pain. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night, for fear that if I did, the words would be too sharp, too full of the things I have not told you.

Tonight, the storm is coming. It roars across the horizon like a beast unleashed, and the wind howls as though it knows the truth. I have seen such storms before, and I have seen men lost to them. I have seen the sea take all, and leave nothing behind. I do not know if I will live through this. But if I do, I must write this, for I fear I may never have the chance again.

**I have not forgotten you, Eliza.**

Not the girl who once ran up the steps of the lighthouse with your father and me, laughing in the wind. Not the child who used to sit by the light and ask me why the stars were so far away. Not the woman I once loved, who left with a heart full of promises and a soul full of secrets.

Certainly. Here's a letter written in the voice of a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper, addressing his estranged daughter with a mix of regret, honesty, and a touch of the old-world bluntness that characterized the era.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it. Five years, Eliza. Five years since you walked out that door and never looked back. I've tried to write, but my hand shakes too much, and my heart's too full of things I can't say without sounding like a fool.

You were a fine girl, Eliza. Not the best, not the worst—just... different. I tried to be a father to you, but I was a lighthouse keeper, not a man of words. I kept the light burning, not the kind that guides ships, but the kind that keeps me from going mad.

Tonight, the storm's coming. I've seen the sky, and it ain't nothin' to joke about. The waves are coming in like they're trying to break the cliffs, and I've seen that kind of fury before. I don't know if I'll make it through the night, and if I don't, I just wanted you to know that I never stopped thinking about you.

You were my pride, my hope. I wanted you to have more than I ever had. I wanted you to be safe, to have a

**My Dearest Lizzie,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I’ve been meaning to write for a long time, but the sea has been so restless these past few weeks, and the wind has howled like a wounded beast. I’ve been up most nights, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and I’ve thought of you more than I care to admit.

Five years, Lizzie. Five years since the last time we spoke. I know you’ve been angry, and I can’t blame you. I was a fool to let things go between us, and I’ve regretted it every day since. I’ve tried to write, but the letters always ended up in the sea, or in the fire, or in the bottom of a bottle. I’m sorry, love. I’m so sorry.

You were only twelve when I left you with your mother. I thought it was for the best. I thought I was doing the right thing. But I was wrong. I should’ve stayed. I should’ve fought for you. I should’ve been there to hold your hand when you were scared. I should’ve been there to tell you I loved you when you needed to hear it.

I know you’ve had your own life, your own troubles. I know you’ve grown up, and I’m sure you’ve moved on. But I still think of you every day. The sea is a cruel

**[From the Lighthouse at the End of the World, 23rd of November, 1889]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast, the sea roaring in a fury that would make even the mightiest of gods bow their heads. I have not seen the dawn for three days, and the lighthouse, which has stood for generations as a beacon of hope to the lost and the lost at sea, now stands in darkness, its light extinguished by the storm.

And yet, I write this not for the lighthouse, nor for the sea, but for you, my dear Eleanor. For five years I have not spoken to you, not even in the pages of the letter that I wrote and then burned, for fear that the words might be read by someone who would not understand the weight of a father's heart.

You were once my light in the darkness, my daughter, my joy, my pride. But pride, I fear, has a way of turning into something far more dangerous. You left me, Eleanor, and you left me in silence, without a word, without a look, as if the very air between us had been poisoned by the weight of your absence.

I have not seen your face in years, nor heard your voice, but I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way you would sit by the fire, your fingers tracing

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a hand that trembles not from the wind, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you left this place, and though I have kept the light burning for you, it has not been the same. The sea has grown colder, the nights longer, and the silence of the cliffs has become a companion of sorts.

Tonight, the storm comes with a fury that speaks of the heavens themselves being in turmoil. I have seen such tempests before, and I know the sea can be cruel. I pray that the winds will not take me before I can reach you. I have prepared the lighthouse, secured the lamps, and made ready for the worst. But I fear that even if I survive, I may not be able to find you.

I have thought often of the last time we spoke, and the words that were left unsaid. I do not know if you still remember the night you stood at the door, your eyes filled with sorrow, and asked me why I had never taken you to see the world beyond these rocks. I told you I had no time, that my duty was to the light. But I have come to see that my duty was also to you, and perhaps I failed in both.

If I do not survive this night, know that I have never stopped thinking of you. I have kept your name on my lips, and your face in my mind, even when the

Ah, my dear child, if you're reading these words, then the storm has come and I have not yet gone. I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the devil himself, and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very earth. I have spent five years in silence, and now, as the heavens weep and the wind screams, I find myself longing to speak to you once more.

Five years, my daughter. Five long, bitter years since I last saw your face, since I last heard your voice. You were so young when you left—no older than a girl of ten. I remember the way your eyes lit up when you first saw me, and the way your laughter echoed through the stone walls of the lighthouse. But then... then you left. You said you needed to find your own way, your own life. And I let you go. I let you go, and I have not spoken to you since.

I was a fool. I thought that silence would mend the wounds, that time would make things right. But time only brings more sorrow. I have watched the sea from these cliffs, night after night, and I have felt the weight of my guilt pressing down upon me like the tide. I have not been able to forgive myself for the pain I caused you, for the love I could not give you when you needed it most.

Now, as the storm approaches, I find myself thinking of you more than ever

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this letter to you with a heart heavy with regret and a soul full of longing. Five long years have passed since I last saw you, and in that time, I have felt the weight of my absence more than ever. Tonight, a storm is coming—one that I fear may not be kind to me, and I wish to leave you with something of my heart, even if I cannot be there to hold it.

You were always my greatest joy and my deepest sorrow. I remember the day you were born, the way your tiny fingers curled around mine, and how your laughter filled our home. You were the light in our lives, and though I have strayed from the path of being the father you needed, I can never forget the love I once had for you.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know that I have wronged you. I was consumed by my duties, by the quiet, solitary life of a lighthouse keeper, and I neglected the bond we once shared. I have not kept in touch with your mother, and I know that this has only deepened the rift between us. I wish I could explain myself, but words have failed me, and I fear that no explanation will ever be enough.

Tonight, the storm is approaching with such ferocity that I am afraid for my life. I have watched the sky darken with clouds that seem to swallow the stars, and I feel the wind

**To My Dearest and Only Daughter,**

**Hearken, child of mine, and lend thine ear to the groan of the wind and the howl of the sea, for I write to thee in the hour of peril, when the very stars above seem to waver and the black tide roars like the wrath of Neptune himself.** 

**Five years hath passed since our last words, and I have not heard from thee, nor seen thy face, save in the flicker of the lantern and the shadow upon the wall.** I have kept the light burning night and day, as it should be, and the sea hath not taken one soul from the rocks since I took up the mantle of keeper. Yet, my heart is heavy, for I feel the weight of the world upon my shoulders, and the burden of a father’s sorrow is a thing no man can bear for long.

**I have heard tell that thou art wed to a man of no great standing, and that thou dwellest in the city, where the smoke of the factories hangs thick in the air and the people speak in tongues I do not understand.** I have heard that thou art not the daughter I raised, but a woman of coldness and pride, who hath turned her back upon her father and the sacred duty of the light. I pray to Saint Peter and the Virgin Mary that this be not true, and that thou art not lost to the ways of the world,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same cold, indifferent silence that has greeted my words for the past five years. I write to you now not from the comfort of my study or the warmth of a hearth, but from the edge of the world—where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the wind bites as if it knows the pain I carry.

It is the night of the storm, and the sky has turned the color of a bruise. The lighthouse, which has stood sentinel for over thirty years, trembles with the weight of the coming tempest. I have lit the beacon, as I have done every night since you left, though tonight its light may not be enough to guide the ships—or perhaps even to guide me.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of letters that were never sent, of thoughts that were never voiced. I have tried, I truly have tried, to find the words that would mend the chasm between us. But I fear that words, like the sea, are fickle things. They can be tossed by the tide of time, or drowned by the weight of unspoken truths.

I know you were angry. I know you thought I had chosen duty over you. And perhaps, in a way, I did. I chose the lighthouse over your childhood, your laughter, your dreams

The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea roars with a voice that has not been heard since the day I last saw you. I sit here in the lighthouse, the lantern flickering like a dying soul, and I feel the weight of years pressing down on me. I have not seen you in five years, not since that last bitter argument, and I do not know if I will see you again.

I suppose I should have written before, but the sea has always been my companion, and my words have always been for the waves and the wind, not for the woman I once called daughter. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I have a feeling it will not be the last one. I have no idea if I will survive it, and I have no one left to tell you what I have come to say.

You were a fine girl, Eliza, and I know I was not the best father. I was too busy with the light, too busy with the sea, and too blind to see the storm that was brewing in your heart. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes full of tears and your voice full of anger. I said nothing, as I always did, and now I am left with silence.

I have no regrets, only memories. I have watched the sea for thirty years, and it has always been there, relentless and unyielding. So it is with you

*April 1st, 1892*

My dear Lila,

I've written this letter many times, but never sent it. I've held it in my hand, trembling, thinking of the words that would cut like the wind through this lighthouse. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones. The sea is restless, the sky is black, and I'm not sure I'll see morning. So I write.

You were ten when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday. You came to the door, your eyes full of questions, your hands clutching a small bundle of clothes. You said you wanted to see the world, to be free, to be someone else. I said yes, because I thought that was what you needed. But I never thought you'd never come back.

I've been alone here for five years. The lighthouse is a tomb of memories. I used to think I'd never be able to live without you. But time, it's a cruel thing. It takes the sharp edges of grief, and dulls them, until you're left with nothing but silence.

I've watched the sea for so long. I've seen the light through the fog, the darkness through the storm, the stars through the night. But I've never seen a light like the one you brought into my life. You were my light, Lila. My little flame. And I

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**From the Edge of the World, Beneath a Shrouded Sky**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast in the night, and the sea roiling as if it were weeping. The lantern above me flickers, its light a feeble match against the storm’s fury. I know not how long I shall endure this night, and if I do not, I pray you will forgive the words that follow.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and five years since the last time I saw your face. Time has worn the world thin, and I have grown old in the silence of this rock. The waves have become my only companion, and the wind my only voice. Yet, in this storm, I have felt a strange stillness — as if the sea itself has paused to whisper your name.

I know you have chosen your path, and I have chosen mine. You left with the promise of a life that would bring you light, and I have remained here, a beacon to those who drift in the dark. But I fear that in your search for the world, you have forgotten the light that once shone in your own heart.

I have not written to you for fear that my words would fall short of the love I hold for you. I have not written because I feared the silence between us would shatter like glass. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From the cliffs of Dunmore, under a sky of wrath and fire,**

I write this with trembling hands, my heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken and the fear of what may come. The wind howls like a beast, the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the land whole. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left me with a final word that cut deeper than any blade. I have not spoken your name, nor have I written to you, not even once. But tonight—tonight, the night the sky turns black and the waves rise like a thousand angry spirits—I must try again.

I have been the lighthouse, you know. A beacon to the lost, a guardian to the sea. But I have also been a father, and a man who has failed you. I did not understand you, and I did not understand myself. I was too proud, too blind, too stubborn to see the love that you carried for me, even when you left. I thought I could wait for you to come back, but I was wrong. You did not come back because you never wanted to.

Certainly. Here's a letter from a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper to his estranged daughter, written in the tone of someone who's seen too much, feels the weight of years, and is facing the end with a mix of regret and resolve:

---

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I've been meaning to write for a long time, but the sea has been too restless, and my hands have been too shaky. The wind howls like a banshee now, and the waves are coming in with a fury that makes me feel like I'm standing on the edge of something I can't control.

You know, I've kept this lighthouse for twenty-three years, and I've seen more storms than I can count. But tonight, it feels different. The sky is blacker than it's ever been, and the sea is screaming. I've got a feeling I won't see dawn. And I'm not sure I'll see you again.

I've been thinking a lot lately. About the days we used to have, before the world got too big for us to share. I remember when you were a little girl, and you'd come running up the steps with your pockets full of stones and your eyes full of wonder. You'd ask me questions about the sea and the stars, and I'd try to answer them as best I could. I thought I was doing right by you, giving you a life

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it’s more likely to find you in a temper, as you’ve always been. Five years, Eliza. Five years since you walked out of our house like the wind had taken your breath away. I’ve tried to write to you, but the postman always says he’s not sure where you’ve gone. I suppose it’s best he doesn’t know. It’s not that I don’t miss you, child—it’s just that I don’t know how to say it without sounding like I’ve been begging for forgiveness all this time.

The sea’s been restless these past few days, and I’ve been watching it more than usual. I think it’s because I’ve been thinking about you. The waves come in so loud, you know, like they’re trying to speak to me. I imagine they’re trying to tell me something. Maybe they’re trying to tell me I should’ve said something sooner, or that I should’ve stayed with you. I don’t know. I’ve been too old and too stubborn to listen, I think.

Tonight, though, the wind is picking up. The sky’s turned a sickly shade of green, and the clouds are rolling in like a sea of black. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, and I’ve been thinking about the storm. I don’t know if I’ll make it through the night,

**[St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight]  
April 3rd, 1876**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this island whole. The night is dark and stormy, and I fear the worst. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I fear that I may not have the chance to do so again.

You must not be surprised if this letter does not reach you. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have learned that the sea does not always give up its secrets. If this letter is found, know that I have not forgotten you, Eliza. I have not forgotten the days when you were a child with a heart full of dreams and a mind full of questions.

I have not spoken to you for five years, and I know that you have been angry. I was wrong to leave you, and I was wrong to let the sea take me from you. I was wrong to think that the work of a lighthouse keeper was more important than the love of a father. I was wrong, and I have paid the price of that mistake with every passing year.

You were a bright light in a dark world, Eliza. You had the gift

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy as the storm that looms beyond the cliffs, for I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet I do so not out of despair, but with the hope that these words may reach you, and that they may serve as a final testament to the love that has never wavered, despite the years of silence between us.

I have watched the sea for many a night, and I have come to know its moods as one might know the face of a beloved. Tonight, it rages with a fury I have not seen in decades. The waves crash against the rocks with a force that shakes the very foundations of this lighthouse. **To My Dearest and Most Beloved Daughter,**

**By the Light of the Lighthouse and the Grace of God, I Write These Words to Thee, Though My Hands Tremble and My Heart Weeps.**

It is now the 12th day of the month of March, in the year of Our Lord 1857, and the wind howls like a banshee through the stones of this lonely keep. The sea, that fickle and cruel mistress, roars with fury, and I fear the tempest that now gathers on the horizon may be the end of me and of this poor lighthouse ere the dawn breaks. Yet, in my hour of greatest need, I have thought of thee, my child, and I have set my quill to parchment to write to thee, though it is but a feeble attempt at penance for the many years I have been silent and unkind.

**First, I must beg thy forgiveness, my dear daughter.**

I know not if thou dost still live, or if thou hast been taken by the sea, or if thou hast found thy way to the city and married some fine gentleman, as I once hoped. *April 18th, 1893*

My dear Elsie,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghost of my wife, the sea roaring as though it's trying to swallow me whole. The lamp is flickering, the oil is low, and I can feel the storm coming—like the one that took her. I can't see the light, but I can feel it. I've been watching the sky all night, and it's not just the weather. It's something deeper. Something inside me.

Five years. Five long, terrible years. I haven't spoken to you since the day you left. I don't know if it was you, or the world, or the sea, that made you walk away. But I've never stopped thinking about you. Not once. I've tried to forget, but the memory of you is like the lighthouse beam—always there, always shining, even when it's dark.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it's more likely to find you in a state of distraction. I've been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea has been a fickle mistress, and the wind has been too loud for me to think straight. But tonight—tonight is different. The sky is dark as pitch, and the waves are growling like a beast that's been roused from its sleep. I've seen storms like this before, but never one that feels so close, so personal.

I suppose I should start by saying I'm sorry. Not just for the way things ended between us, but for the way I've been since you left. I've been a fool, Eliza, and I've been a man who's let the sea take more of me than it should. I know I've been distant, and I know I've said things I didn't mean, but I've always meant to make it right. I just... I didn't know how.

You were always my favorite thing in the world, you know. Even when I was too stubborn to say it.
